title: Home
text:
Video game music has evolved dramatically over time. Early games were shaped by hardware limitations and relied on short looping melodies. Modern games, by contrast, feature larger soundtracks with longer and more varied compositions.
This project investigates how the structure and scale of video game soundtracks changed across technological eras.



How has the scale of video game soundtracks changed across technological eras?




how many tracks different games contain
how soundtrack runtime changes over time
how track-length distributions vary across eras
what these patterns suggest about technological change

text:

This project investigates how the structure and scale of video game soundtracks changed across technological eras.

title: Home

text:
how many tracks different games contain

what these patterns suggest about technological change

title: Conclusion
text:
Main Findings

newer games tend to include more tracks
soundtrack runtime increased across technological eras
early games relied more heavily on short looping musical structures

title: Conclusion
text:
Interpretation
These patterns suggest that technological change shaped not just audio quality, but also the structure and scale of game music itself.

title: Conclusion
text:
Future Work
Future research could examine:

more games per era
local audio feature extraction
emotional or stylistic differences across franchises

title: Analysis
text:
Methods
To compare soundtrack structure across eras, I use:

total number of tracks per game
total runtime per game
average track length
track-length distributions

These measures help show how soundtrack structure expanded as gaming technology evolved


Number of Tracks per Game Over Time

library(ggplot2)

Warning: package 'ggplot2' was built under R version 4.4.3

library(dplyr)

Warning: package 'dplyr' was built under R version 4.4.3



Attaching package: 'dplyr'


The following objects are masked from 'package:stats':

    filter, lag


The following objects are masked from 'package:base':

    intersect, setdiff, setequal, union

df <- read.csv("video_game_ost_cleaned_with_seconds.csv")

game_summary <- df |>
  group_by(game, year, era) |>
  summarise(
    total_tracks = n(),
    total_runtime_sec = sum(Duration_sec, na.rm = TRUE),
    avg_track_length = mean(Duration_sec, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  mutate(total_runtime_min = total_runtime_sec / 60)

game_summary

# A tibble: 13 × 7
   game               year era   total_tracks total_runtime_sec avg_track_length
   <chr>             <int> <chr>        <int>             <int>            <dbl>
 1 Halo               2001 CD_E…           11            116280           10571.
 2 Halo               2002 CD_E…            9             44400            4933.
 3 Mega Man           1987 Chip…           16            127260            7954.
 4 Mega Man           1991 Chip…            2             15780            7890 
 5 Metal Gear Solid   1998 CD_E…           19            194760           10251.
 6 Minecraft          2011 Mode…           24            211800            8825 
 7 Skyrim             2011 Mode…           15            210600           14040 
 8 Sonic The Hedgeh…  1991 Chip…            7             46560            6651.
 9 Super Mario 64     1997 CD_E…           20            121500            6075 
10 Super Mario Bros   1986 Chip…           16            110700            6919.
11 The Last of Us     2013 Mode…           15            119100            7940 
12 The Legend of Ze…  1986 Chip…           12             67080            5590 
13 The Legend of Ze…  1998 CD_E…           20            161280            8064 
# ℹ 1 more variable: total_runtime_min <dbl>




Total Soundtrack Runtime Over Time

ggplot(game_summary, aes(x = year, y = total_tracks)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "Number of Tracks per Game Over Time",
    x = "Year",
    y = "Number of Tracks"
  )

`geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'











Runtime by Era

ggplot(game_summary, aes(x = era, y = total_runtime_min)) +
  geom_boxplot() +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "Soundtrack Runtime by Era",
    x = "Era",
    y = "Runtime (minutes)"
  )










Distribution of Track Lengths

ggplot(df, aes(x = Duration_sec)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 40) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Track Lengths",
    x = "Track Duration (seconds)",
    y = "Count"
  )

title: Data
text:
This dataset was manually constructed from representative video game soundtrack tracklists across multiple eras.

library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)


Attaching package: 'dplyr'


The following objects are masked from 'package:stats':

    filter, lag


The following objects are masked from 'package:base':

    intersect, setdiff, setequal, union

df <- read.csv("video_game_ost_cleaned_with_seconds.csv")


Dataset
This dataset was manually constructed from representative video game soundtrack tracklists across multiple eras.
Each row represents one track and includes

game
year
era
track title
duration in seconds
